title: Boxty
subtitle: Traditional Irish potato pancakes made with raw and mashed potato
excerpt: Boxty is the Irish potato pancake made from a combination of raw grated potato and mashed potato, bound with flour and buttermilk. The raw potato gives it a slight bite and a denser texture than a standard pancake. It is fried until golden and eaten with butter.
sourceNotes: Traditional Irish dish from the Ulster and Connacht regions, associated with Northern Ireland and the border counties. Boxty appears in Irish food culture from at least the 19th century. The name likely derives from the Irish 'bochtan' or 'arán bocht tí' (poor-house bread). The traditional rhyme 'Boxty on the griddle, boxty in the pan' attests to its long history.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze cooked boxty between sheets of baking paper for up to 2 months. Reheat in a dry frying pan.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The batter can be made 30 minutes ahead. Do not make too far in advance as the grated potato will discolour.

content.text: Boxty uses two forms of potato: raw grated, which gives texture and density, and mashed, which binds the pancake together. The key is to squeeze as much liquid as possible from the grated potato before mixing: if you leave the water in, the batter will be too loose and the pancakes will not hold together in the pan. Use a floury potato for the best result.

items.prepNote: floury variety, peeled: half grated raw, half boiled and mashed
for frying, plus extra to serve

content.text: Prepare the potato and make the batter
Grate 200 g of the raw peeled potato on the coarse side of a box grater. Gather in a clean cloth and wring out as much liquid as possible. The potato should feel almost dry.
Boil the remaining 200 g of potato until tender. Drain well and mash until smooth with no lumps. Allow to cool slightly.
Mix the grated and mashed potato together in a large bowl. Add the flour, baking powder, and salt. Stir in the buttermilk and mix to a thick batter. Leave to rest for 10 minutes.

Melt a little butter in a heavy frying pan over a medium heat. Drop large spoonfuls of batter into the pan, pressing lightly to form rounds about 1 cm thick. Cook for 3 to 4 minutes per side until deep golden and cooked through.
Keep warm while you cook the remaining boxty. Serve with plenty of butter.

items.symptom: The boxty falls apart in the pan
items.cause: Too much moisture left in the grated potato, or the batter was too thin.
items.fix: Squeeze the grated potato very thoroughly. The batter should hold its shape when dropped from a spoon.

items.symptom: The inside is still raw after the outside is golden
items.cause: Boxty made too thick, or heat too high.
items.fix: Keep to about 1 cm thick and cook over a medium rather than high heat. Cover the pan for the first 2 minutes to trap steam.

items.cause: The grated potato was left to sit and has oxidised.
items.fix: Grate the potato immediately before mixing and work quickly. A little discolouration is normal and does not affect the taste.

content.text: Boxty is native to Ulster and Connacht. It has been made in Ireland since at least the 1800s, when the potato was the staple food. It appears in an old rhyme: 'Boxty on the griddle, boxty in the pan, if you can't make boxty, you'll never get a man.' It fell out of use in the 20th century but has come back in recent years.
